coredata.dc:title: Quasicrystalline phase formation in Zr–Ti–Nb–Cu–Ni–(Al) metallic glasses

Eckert, J.
Mickel, C.
Schubert-Bischoff, P.
Breitzke, H.
Lüders, K.
Schultz, L.
coredata.dc:description:
The crystallization behavior of (Zr0.616Ti0.087Nb0.027Cu0.15Ni0.12)100−
x Al x melt-spun glassy ribbons with x = 7.5 and 0 has been investigated. The glasses devitrify by the precipitation of an icosahedral quasi-crystalline phase in the first step of the crystallization process, indicating that the absence of Al does not hinder quasicrystal formation in the present alloys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metallic glass
Quasicrystals
Transmission electron microscopy
Thermal analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Constant-rate heating DSC (40K/min) scans of the (Zr0.616Ti0.087 Nb0.027Cu0.15Ni0.12)100−
x Al x (x = 7.5 and 0) as-spun ribbons.
XRD patterns (Co–Kα radiation) for the ribbon with x = 7.5 in the as-spun state and after heating at 40K/min up to the first (730K) and second (800K) exothermic DSC events.
XRD patterns (Co–Kα radiation) for the ribbon with x = 0 in the as-spun state and after heating at 40K/min up to the first (690K) and second (745K) exothermic DSC events.

(a) Bright-field TEM image of the (Zr0.616Ti0.087 Nb0.027Cu0.15Ni0.12)100−
x Al x ribbon with x = 0 heated to completion of the first exothermic DSC event. (b–d) Electron nanodiffraction patterns taken from the precipitated particles exhibiting five-, three- and two-fold rotational symmetries.

Schematic free energy diagram as a function of Al and Zr content of the amorphous-to-quasicrystalline transformation in the (Zr0.616Ti0.087Nb0.027Cu0.15 Ni0.12)100−
x Al x melt-spun glassy ribbons with x = 7.5 and 0.

article.ce:title: Quasicrystalline phase formation in Zr–Ti–Nb–Cu–Ni–(Al) metallic glasses

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The crystallization behavior of (Zr0.616Ti0.087Nb0.027Cu0.15Ni0.12)100−
x Al x melt-spun glassy ribbons with x = 7.5 and 0 has been investigated. The glasses devitrify by the precipitation of an icosahedral quasi-crystalline phase in the first step of the crystallization process, indicating that the absence of Al does not hinder quasicrystal formation in the present alloys.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the first report on quasicrystalline (QC) phase formation by Köster et al. [1,2] in the first stage of crystallization of Zr65Cu17.5Ni10Al7.5 and Zr69.5Cu12Ni11Al7.5 metallic glasses, Al is an element often present in Zr-based glassy alloys that form QC upon partial devitrification [3]. However, so far, the role of Al in the formation of quasicrystals has not been completely clarified and the investigations performed have led to different results depending on the alloy composition. For instance, Murty et al. [4] have shown that, in contrast to the ZrCuAl glassy alloy, the ZrCu metallic glass does not undergo QC formation, therefore, demonstrating that Al is necessary for QC formation in that particular system. On the other hand, the discovery of QC precipitation in binary and ternary alloys, e.g. Zr–Pd, Zr–Pt [5] and Zr–Ni–M (M = Pd, Pt, Au) [6], reveals that Al is not necessary for QC formation in these systems. Except a few examples, such as the Zr-based alloys containing Be [7] or the Zr55Cu20Ti15Ni10 metallic glass [8], when the number of elements is increased and the alloy becomes more complex, Al is generally found among the alloy components [3,9,10]. This suggests that in multicomponent Zr-based alloys the presence of Al, even though not always necessary, might have a positive effect on the formation of the quasicrystalline phase.

In order to analyze this feature, in this paper, we report on the effect of Al on the crystallization behavior of (Zr0.616Ti0.087Nb0.027Cu0.15Ni0.12)100−
x Al x melt-spun glassy ribbons with x = 7.5 and 0. The thermal stability and the crystallization behavior were investigated with the aim of clarifying the influence of Al on the mechanism of the amorphous-to-quasicrystalline phase transformation. The structure of the phase precipitated in the first crystallization event has been examined by X-ray diffraction and transmission electron microscopy in order to prove its icosahedral structure.

Pre-alloyed ingots with nominal composition (Zr0.616Ti0.087Nb0.027Cu0.15Ni0.12)100−
x Al x (x = 7.5 and 0) were prepared from the pure elements (purity >99.9wt.%) by arc melting in a titanium-gettered argon atmosphere. The ingot was remelted several times in order to achieve homogeneity in composition. Ribbons with a cross-section of about 0.05mm × 3mm were prepared in a single-roller Bühler melt spinner under an argon atmosphere at a wheel velocity of 14.3m/s and at an injection temperature of about 1400K. The amount of oxygen, evaluated by carrier gas hot extraction using a Leco TC-436DR analyzer, was found to be about 0.06wt.%. The microstructure was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Philips PW 1050 diffractometer (Co–Kα radiation) and by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a Philips CM 20 microscope. The specimens for TEM observation were prepared by the wedge technique [11]. The thermal stability was investigated by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) at 40K/min heating rate with a Perkin-Elmer DSC7 under a continuous flow of purified argon.

Fig. 1 shows the constant-rate heating DSC (40K/min) scans of the (Zr0.616Ti0.087Nb0.027Cu0.15Ni0.12)100−
x Al x as-spun ribbons with x = 7.5 and 0. The DSC curves exhibit a distinct glass transition, followed by a supercooled liquid region before two crystallization events occur at higher temperature. The onset temperature of the glass transition, T g, estimated as the point of intersection between the linearly extrapolated curve below the transition with the steepest tangent of the rise in the heat flow signal [12], is 646 and 599K for the alloys with x = 7.5 and x = 0, respectively. The crystallization temperatures Tx
1 and Tx
2, defined as the onset temperatures of the first and second exothermic DSC peaks, respectively, are 698 and 747K for x = 7.5 and, 641 and 725K for x = 0. The supercooled liquid region, ΔTx = Tx
1 − Tx
2, that corresponds to the stability of the supercooled liquid against crystallization, is 50K for the alloy with x = 7.5 and 42K for the Al-free ribbon. The enthalpies of crystallization related to the first (ΔH 1) and second (ΔH 2) exothermic DSC peaks are 32.5 and 22.6J/g, respectively, for the alloy with x = 7.5 and 32.5 and = 22.6J/g for the alloy with x = 0.

When partially crystallized materials are considered, a fundamental parameter that has to be taken into account is the temperature range of stability of the phase formed, which, in the present case, is the temperature interval between the first crystallization temperature, where the QC from, and the subsequent crystallization event (ΔTx
2 = Tx
2 − Tx
1). For the alloy with x = 7.5, this interval was found to be about 51K while for the alloy with x = 0, it increases up to 84K, which is 20–40K wider compared with other multicomponent QC-forming alloys, such as Zr65Al7.5Ni10Cu12.5X5 metallic glasses (X = Ag, Pd, Au, Pt, V, Nb and Ta) [13].

The absence of Al strongly affects the devitrification by decreasing the stability of the alloy against crystallization by about 50K, giving rise to a slight decrease of the supercooled liquid region by about 8K and to a strong increase of the temperature range of stability of the QC phase by about 30K. Therefore, if one is interested in the amorphous material it will be best to choose the alloy with x = 7.5, in which the glassy phase is more stable. On the other hand, if the partially crystallized material is required, the Al-free ribbon is the better choice, since it is characterized by a wider temperature range of stability of the primary crystallized phase.

With the aim of investigating the structural evolution during the crystallization process, pieces of the (Zr0.616Ti0.087 Nb0.027Cu0.15Ni0.12)100−
x Al x as-spun ribbons were annealed in the DSC by continuous heating at 40K/min up to different temperatures through the exothermic events and then cooled to room temperature at 100K/min. The phases formed after annealing were identified by XRD and the patterns are shown in Figs. 2 and 3
for the alloys with x = 7.5 and x = 0, respectively, together with the patterns of the as-prepared samples. Both as-spun ribbons exhibit the typical broad maxima characteristic for amorphous materials and no traces of crystalline phases, indicating that they are in the amorphous state. This implies that Al is not necessary for glass formation in the present alloy. The phase precipitated in the ribbon with x = 7.5 heated up to the completion of the first crystallization event (730K) was previously identified by XRD and TEM as an icosahedral quasicrystalline phase with 5–10nm grain size and with a quasi-lattice constant, a q, of 0.479nm [10,14]. Similarly, when the sample with x = 0 is heated up to 690K, i.e. above the first crystallization peak, the glassy phase devitrifies by precipitation of an icosahedral quasicrystalline phase that can be indexed according to the scheme of Bancel et al. [15], using the 12 vectors pointing to the vertices of an icosahedron. The peak at 2θ = 41.7° was taken as the fundamental (100,000) reciprocal lattice vector. The absence of Al induces sharpening of the QC diffraction peaks, suggesting an increase of the QC grain size to about 10–20nm as estimated from the XRD data and shifts the peak towards lower scattering angles. The shift of the QC diffraction peaks is linked with an increase of the quasi-lattice constant, a q. In fact, the quasi-lattice constant of the alloy with x = 0, calculated from the four strongest diffraction peaks, was found to be 0.481nm, approximately 0.4% larger than in the ribbon with x = 7.5. Such a small change in a q allows to rule out structural differences that would give rise to larger variations [16]. With decreasing Al content from 7.5 to 0 at.%, only about 40% of the atoms that substitute Al have similar or smaller atomic radius, while the remaining part (i.e. Zr) has a larger atomic radius. This suggests that the increase of the quasi-lattice constant is most likely due to the substitution of Al by Zr that gives rise to less dense atomic packing. Heating the ribbon with x = 7.5 up to the completion of the second exothermic DSC peak (800K) leads to the formation of a tetragonal NiZr2-type phase (space group I4/mcm) with lattice constants a = 0.651nm and c = 0.524nm, and a minor amount of fcc NiTi2-type big cube phase (space group Fd3m) with lattice constant a = 1.221nm. The same phases were observed in the Al-free ribbon heated above the second crystallization peak (745K). However, the lattice constants of the tetragonal NiZr2-type phase (a = 0.655nm and c = 0.525nm) and of the big cube phase (a = 1.224nm) are larger than in the alloy with x = 7.5. Therefore, besides the increase of the quasi-lattice constant, the substitution of Al with elements having a larger atomic radius (i.e. Zr) induces also an increase of the lattice constants of the crystalline compounds, similar as it was found for the increase of the quasi-lattice constant with decreasing Al content. It is worth noticing that in both ribbons, no trace of QC was observed after complete transformation. This indicates that the quasicrystalline phase is in a metastable state and transforms into crystalline intermetallic compounds in the second crystallization event.

Fig. 4(a) displays a bright-field TEM image of the Al-free ribbon after heating to the completion of the first crystallization event, revealing a homogeneous distribution of particles with a size on the order of 10–20nm, which confirms the grain size estimation based on the XRD data. The electron nanodiffraction patterns taken from the precipitated particles are presented in Fig. 4(b–d). The patterns exhibit five-, three- and two-fold rotational symmetries, which are the symmetries required for the icosahedral point group. The distances between the diffraction spots along the radial directions in Fig. 4(b) are related each to other by τ = (1 + √5)/2, the golden mean. In addition, the angles between the spots are 2π/10 = 36°. This confirms the results from XRD, corroborating that the particles precipitated in the first crystallization event have an icosahedral structure, and that the absence of Al yields larger QC grains.

The amorphous-to-quasicrystalline transformation in the alloy with x = 7.5 is characterized by the redistribution of Al and Zr between the amorphous and the QC phase [17]. Al is slightly depleted and Zr is slightly enriched in the nanosized quasicrystalline precipitates, revealing that QC formation is a primary transformation (i.e. crystallization of a phase with a different composition than the amorphous matrix). A schematic free energy diagram as a function of Al and Zr content for this transformation is presented in Fig. 5 . When the amorphous phase with composition C 0 is annealed, its free energy is lowered from A to B by primary crystallization of the QC phase from the amorphous matrix with a composition shift of both the QC phase (C 1) and the remaining amorphous phase (C 2). With decreasing Al content, the transformation may eventually develop towards a polymorphous crystallization mode (i.e. crystallization of a phase with the same composition as the amorphous matrix). For example, the amorphous phase may crystallize polymorphously lowering its free energy from G to H without change in composition. A similar mechanism may hold for the Al-free alloy. The change from a primary to a polymorphous mechanism, that does not require long-range diffusion but only local topological atomic rearrangements [18], should facilitate the devitrification of the amorphous phase and give rise to the precipitation of QC with larger grain size. In fact, since the finest microstructure should be obtained during primary crystallization [19], it is expected that polymorphous crystallization, whose growth is controlled by interfacial diffusion [19], induces formation of larger QC precipitates. Both these features have been observed in this work. The stability against crystallization of the amorphous phase was found to be reduced in the Al-free ribbon, indicating an easier crystallization, and larger QC grain sizes were found in the TEM investigations.

The scenario presented in Fig. 5 is of course a simplified view of the amorphous-to-quasicrystalline phase transformation. With decreasing Al content the reaction may still be primary (e.g. E → F transformation), but the difference of composition between the arising QC and the parent amorphous phase is lower and consequently, the amount of Al that diffuses from the amorphous to the QC phase is reduced. As already mentioned, primary crystallization is controlled by long-range diffusion. Therefore, even when the transformation is not fully polymorphous and element partitioning takes place, the less pronounced diffusion due to the reduction of Al content should lead in any case to a higher growth rate and, therefore, to larger QC grain sizes as observed by TEM analysis.

The variation of Al strongly affects the crystallization behavior of (Zr0.616Ti0.087Nb0.027Cu0.15Ni0.12)100−
x Al x (x = 7.5 and 0) melt-spun glassy ribbons. With decreasing Al content from 7.5 to 0 at.% the stability against crystallization of the amorphous phase decreases by about 50K together with a slight decrease of the supercooled liquid region by about 8K, indicating that Al is essential for improving the thermal stability of the glassy phase. Regardless of the amount of Al, the devitrification of the glassy ribbons is characterized by the formation of a nanosized icosahedral quasicrystalline phase during the first stage of the crystallization process, demonstrating that Al is not necessary for quasicrystal formation in the present alloys. In addition, lowering the Al content has a positive effect on the temperature range of stability of the quasicrystalline phase that increases by about 30K and yields larger QC grain sizes upon devitrification, probably as a consequence of a change from a primary to a polymorphous crystallization mechanism.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank M. Frey and H. Schulze for technical assistance, J. Acker and W. Gruner for chemical analysis, J. Thomas for help with the TEM investigations, and B. Bartusch, S. Deledda, and U. Kühn for stimulating discussions. This work was supported by the German Science Foundation under grants Ec 111/10-1,2 and Lu 217/17-1,2.
